title: Meningitis B Vaccine Scheme Expands to Year 11 Students Amid Kent Outbreak
description: The UK Health Security Agency has expanded the meningitis B vaccination program to include Year 11 pupils in Kent as confirmed cases fall to 20.
lede: The UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA) is expanding the meningitis B vaccination program to Year 11 pupils at four schools in Kent following an outbreak linked to a local nightclub. Confirmed cases have fallen to 20, down from the peak, as officials work to ensure long-term protection for students in affected areas.

sections.heading: What Happened
paragraphs: Health officials have confirmed that the meningitis B vaccination scheme in Kent will now cover Year 11 pupils at four schools with known or suspected cases. This decision follows the initial vaccination rollout for University of Kent students, which began on March 18.

According to the UKHSA, the number of confirmed meningitis cases stands at 20 as of Monday afternoon, with an additional three under investigation. This represents a decrease from the previous day, which saw 20 confirmed cases and nine under investigation.

The outbreak appears closely linked to a local venue. The UKHSA reports that 87% of patients attended Club Chemistry in Canterbury between March 5 and March 7. The median age of those affected is 19, and while the majority are students, five are not.

sections.heading: Why It Matters
paragraphs: The expansion of the vaccination program is described by officials as a precautionary measure intended to provide long-term protection to vulnerable populations. Given the severity of meningitis B, halting the spread among young adults and teenagers is critical.

Tragically, the outbreak has already claimed two lives, including 18-year-old Juliette Kenny and another University of Kent student. All patients in the Kent outbreak required hospital admission, with nine admitted to intensive care, where four currently remain.

sections.heading: The Bigger Picture
paragraphs: The strain responsible for the outbreak has been identified as a recently emerged subtype of group B meningococci, present in England since 2020. UKHSA analysis indicates that the genome of these bacteria has multiple potentially significant genetic differences compared to closely related genomes.

Despite these genetic variations, early analysis provides strong reassurance. Professor Robin May, chief scientific officer at the UKHSA, confirmed that the strain responds to existing antibiotics and the current meningitis B vaccine.

sections.heading: What's Next
paragraphs: Health officials and the research community are conducting intensive laboratory investigations to understand the strain better. They aim to determine how bacterial behavior, social and environmental conditions, and population immunity influenced the spread of the outbreak.

As the vaccination rollout continues for Year 11 and university students, public health monitoring in Kent will remain stringent to prevent further infections and manage any new suspected cases swiftly.
